* France (and Navarre until 1830): + 1830 ([July Revolution](/wiki/July_Revolution "July Revolution"))   + 1848 ([February Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution_of_1848 "French Revolution of 1848")) * Parma: 1859 ([Annexed](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma#Post-Napoleonic_era "Duchy of Parma") by the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)")) * Two Sicilies: 1861 ([Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification"))

(English: [/ˈbʊərbən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), also [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈbɔːrbɒn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); French: [[buʁbɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty") that originated in the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") as a branch of the [Capetian dynasty](/wiki/Capetian_dynasty "Capetian dynasty"), the royal House of France. The **House of Bourbon** (English: [/ˈbʊərbən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), also [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈbɔːrbɒn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); French: [[buʁbɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a [dynasty](/wiki/Dynasty "Dynasty") that originated in the [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") as a branch of the [Capetian dynasty](/wiki/Capetian_dynasty "Capetian dynasty"), the royal House of France. Bourbon kings first ruled France and [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") in the 16th century. A branch descended from the French Bourbons came to rule [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") in the 18th century and is the current [Spanish royal family](/wiki/Spanish_royal_family "Spanish royal family"). Other branches, descended from the Spanish Bourbons, held thrones in [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), [Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"), and [Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duchy of Parma and Piacenza"). Today, Spain and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") have monarchs from the House of Bourbon.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

The royal Bourbons originated in 1272, when [Robert](/wiki/Robert,_Count_of_Clermont "Robert, Count of Clermont"), the youngest son of King [Louis IX of France](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France"), married the heiress of the [lordship of Bourbon](/wiki/Sire_de_Bourbon "Sire de Bourbon").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) The house continued for three centuries as a [cadet branch](/wiki/Cadet_branch "Cadet branch"), serving as nobles under the direct Capetian and [Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois") kings.

The senior line of the House of Bourbon became extinct in the male line in 1527 with the death of Duke [Charles III of Bourbon](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Bourbon "Charles III of Bourbon"). This made the junior [Bourbon-Vendôme](/wiki/Bourbon-Vend%C3%B4me "Bourbon-Vendôme") branch the genealogically senior branch of the House of Bourbon. In 1589, at the death of [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"), the House of Valois became extinct in the male line. Under the [Salic law](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law"), the head of the House of Bourbon, as the senior representative of the senior-surviving branch of the Capetian dynasty ([first prince of the blood](/wiki/Prince_du_sang#Monsieur_le_Prince "Prince du sang")), became King of France as [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Bourbon monarchs then united to France the part of the [Kingdom of Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") north of the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"), which Henry's father had acquired by marriage in 1555, ruling both until the 1792 overthrow of the monarchy during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Restored briefly in 1814 and definitively in 1815 after the fall of the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"), the senior line of the Bourbons was finally overthrown in the [July Revolution](/wiki/July_Revolution "July Revolution") of 1830. A cadet Bourbon branch, the [House of Orléans](/wiki/House_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "House of Orléans"), then ruled for 18 years (1830–1848), until it too was overthrown during the [French Revolution of 1848](/wiki/French_Revolution_of_1848 "French Revolution of 1848").

The [House of Condé](/wiki/Princes_of_Cond%C3%A9 "Princes of Condé") was a cadet branch of the Bourbons descended from an uncle of [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France"), and the [House of Conti](/wiki/Princes_of_Conti "Princes of Conti") was a cadet line of the Condé branch. Both houses, recognized as [princes of the blood](/wiki/Princes_of_the_blood "Princes of the blood"), were prominent French noble families, well known for their participation in French affairs, even during exile in the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), until their respective extinctions in 1830 and 1814. Consequently, since the extinction of the [Capetian House of Courtenay](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Courtenay "Capetian House of Courtenay") in 1733, the Bourbons are the only extant legitimate branch of the House of Capet. Although illegitimate, the [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza") traces its line to the House of Capet via descent from [Robert II of France](/wiki/Robert_II_of_France "Robert II of France") through the [First House of Burgundy](/wiki/House_of_Burgundy "House of Burgundy"), then through the [Portuguese House of Burgundy](/wiki/Portuguese_House_of_Burgundy "Portuguese House of Burgundy"). [Peter I of Portugal](/wiki/Peter_I_of_Portugal "Peter I of Portugal") fathered an illegitimate son – [John I of Portugal](/wiki/John_I_of_Portugal "John I of Portugal"), founder of the [House of Aviz](/wiki/House_of_Aviz "House of Aviz") – who in turn fathered an illegitimate son named [Afonso](/wiki/Afonso_I,_Duke_of_Braganza "Afonso I, Duke of Braganza"), who in turn founded the extant House of Braganza.

In 1700, upon the death of King [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain"), the Spanish [Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") became extinct in the male line. Under the will of the childless Charles II, the second grandson of King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France was named as his successor, to preclude the union of the thrones of France and Spain. The prince, then Duke of Anjou, became [Philip V of Spain](/wiki/Philip_V_of_Spain "Philip V of Spain").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Permanent separation of the French and Spanish thrones was secured when France and Spain [ratified](/wiki/Ratification "Ratification") Philip's [renunciation](/wiki/Renunciation "Renunciation"), for himself and his descendants, of the French throne in the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") in 1713, and similar arrangements later kept the Spanish throne separate from those of [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), [Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") and [Parma](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duchy of Parma and Piacenza"). The Spanish House of Bourbon (rendered in Spanish as *Borbón* [[boɾˈβon]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")) has been overthrown and restored several times, reigning 1700–1808, 1813–1868, 1875–1931, and since 1975. Bourbons ruled in [Naples](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Naples "List of monarchs of Naples") from 1734 to 1806 and in [Sicily](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Sicily "List of monarchs of Sicily") from 1735 to 1816, as well as in a unified [Kingdom of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies") from 1816 to 1861. They also ruled in [Parma](/wiki/Duke_of_Parma "Duke of Parma") from 1731 to 1735, 1748 to 1802 and 1847 to 1859, in the [Kingdom of Etruria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Etruria "Kingdom of Etruria") from 1802 to 1807, and in the [Duchy of Lucca](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lucca "Duchy of Lucca") from 1814 to 1847.

Grand Duchess [Charlotte of Luxembourg](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Luxembourg "Charlotte of Luxembourg") married [Prince Felix of Bourbon-Parma](/wiki/Prince_Felix_of_Bourbon-Parma "Prince Felix of Bourbon-Parma"), and thus her successors, who have reigned in Luxembourg since her abdication in 1964, have also been members of the House of Bourbon. [Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil](/wiki/Isabel,_Princess_Imperial_of_Brazil "Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil"), regent for her father, Emperor [Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil"), married a cadet of the Orléans line and thus their descendants, known as the [Orléans-Braganza](/wiki/Brazilian_imperial_family "Brazilian imperial family"), were in the line of succession to the Brazilian throne and expected to ascend its throne had the monarchy not been abolished by a [coup in 1889](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)").[[3]](#cite_note-4) All legitimate, living members of the House of Bourbon, including its cadet branches, are direct [agnatic](/wiki/Agnatic "Agnatic") descendants of Henry IV through his son [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII") of France.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Chateau_de_Bourbon_l%27Archambault_01.jpg/250px-Chateau_de_Bourbon_l%27Archambault_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chateau_de_Bourbon_l%27Archambault_01.jpg)

The castle of [Bourbon-l'Archambault](/wiki/Bourbon-l%27Archambault "Bourbon-l'Archambault")

The pre-Capetian House of [Bourbon](/wiki/Bourbonnais "Bourbonnais") was a [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") family, dating at least from the beginning of the 13th century, when the [estate](/wiki/Family_seat "Family seat") of Bourbon was ruled by the [Sire de Bourbon](/wiki/Sire_de_Bourbon "Sire de Bourbon") who was a vassal of the [King of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France"). The term House of Bourbon ("Maison de Bourbon") is sometimes used to refer to this [first house](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Premi%C3%A8re_maison_de_Bourbon "fr:Première maison de Bourbon") and the [House of Bourbon-Dampierre](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Dampierre "House of Bourbon-Dampierre"), the second family to rule the [seigneury](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism").[[4]](#cite_note-5)

In 1272, [Robert, Count of Clermont](/wiki/Robert,_Count_of_Clermont "Robert, Count of Clermont"), sixth and youngest son of King [Louis IX of France](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France"), married [Beatrix of Bourbon](/wiki/Beatrix_of_Burgundy,_Dame_de_Bourbon "Beatrix of Burgundy, Dame de Bourbon"), heiress to the [lordship of Bourbon](/wiki/Sire_de_Bourbon "Sire de Bourbon") and member of the House of Bourbon-Dampierre.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Their son [Louis](/wiki/Louis_I,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Louis I, Duke of Bourbon") was made [Duke of Bourbon](/wiki/Duke_of_Bourbon "Duke of Bourbon") in 1327. His descendant, the [Constable of France](/wiki/Constable_of_France "Constable of France") [Charles de Bourbon](/wiki/Charles_III,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Charles III, Duke of Bourbon"), was the last of the senior Bourbon line when he died in 1527. Because he chose to fight under the banner of [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") and lived in exile from France, his title was discontinued after his death.

The remaining line of Bourbons henceforth descended from [James I, Count of La Marche](/wiki/James_I,_Count_of_La_Marche "James I, Count of La Marche"), the younger son of [Louis I, Duke of Bourbon](/wiki/Louis_I,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Louis I, Duke of Bourbon").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) With the death of his grandson [James II, Count of La Marche](/wiki/James_II,_Count_of_La_Marche "James II, Count of La Marche") in 1438, the senior line of the Count of La Marche became extinct. All future Bourbons would descend from James II's younger brother, [Louis](/wiki/Louis,_Count_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Louis, Count of Vendôme"), who became the [Count of Vendôme](/wiki/Count_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Count of Vendôme") through his mother's inheritance.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) In 1525, at the death of [Charles IV, Duke of Alençon](/wiki/Charles_IV,_Duke_of_Alen%C3%A7on "Charles IV, Duke of Alençon"), all of the [princes of the blood royal](/wiki/Prince_du_sang "Prince du sang") were Bourbons; all remaining members of the House of Valois were members of the king's immediate family.

In 1514, [Charles, Count of Vendôme](/wiki/Charles,_Duke_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Charles, Duke of Vendôme") had his title raised to [Duke of Vendôme](/wiki/Duke_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Duke of Vendôme"). His son [Antoine](/wiki/Antoine_of_Navarre "Antoine of Navarre") became King of [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre"), on the northern side of the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"), by marriage in 1555.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Two of Antoine's younger brothers were Cardinal Archbishop [Charles de Bourbon](/wiki/Charles,_Cardinal_de_Bourbon_(born_1523) "Charles, Cardinal de Bourbon (born 1523)") and the French and Huguenot general [Louis de Bourbon, 1st Prince of Condé](/wiki/Louis,_Prince_of_Cond%C3%A9_(1530%E2%80%931569) "Louis, Prince of Condé (1530–1569)"). Louis' male-line descendants, the [Princes de Condé](/wiki/Princes_of_Cond%C3%A9 "Princes of Condé"), survived until 1830. Finally, in 1589, the [House of Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois") died out and Antoine's son [Henry III of Navarre](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") became Henry IV of France.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3)

**Family from India's claim to be a branch and their claim to The "Throne of France"**

* [Bourbons of India](/wiki/Bourbons_of_India "Bourbons of India"), claim to be descendants of [Charles III, Duke of Bourbon](/wiki/Charles_III,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Charles III, Duke of Bourbon"), of the first House of Bourbon-Montpensier.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-amp.theguardian.com-7)[[7]](#cite_note-The_next_King_of_France?_An_Indian!-8)[[8]](#cite_note-Bourbon_of_Indian_vintage-9)

As per the latest research carried out by [Prince Michael of Greece](/wiki/Prince_Michael_of_Greece "Prince Michael of Greece") and incorporated in his historical novel, *Le Rajah Bourbon*,[[9]](#cite_note-10) [Balthazar Napoleon IV de Bourbon](/wiki/Balthazar_Napoleon_IV_de_Bourbon "Balthazar Napoleon IV de Bourbon") from India is the senior heir in line to the [French throne](/wiki/List_of_heirs_to_the_French_throne "List of heirs to the French throne").[[6]](#cite_note-amp.theguardian.com-7)[[7]](#cite_note-The_next_King_of_France?_An_Indian!-8)[[8]](#cite_note-Bourbon_of_Indian_vintage-9)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

### Rise of Henry IV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rise of Henry IV")]

Main article: [Henry IV of France's succession](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France%27s_succession "Henry IV of France's succession")

The first Bourbon [king of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France") was [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) He was born on 13 December 1553 in the [Kingdom of Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre"). [Antoine de Bourbon](/wiki/Antoine_de_Bourbon "Antoine de Bourbon"), his father, was a ninth-generation descendant of King [Louis IX of France](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) [Jeanne d'Albret](/wiki/Jeanne_d%27Albret "Jeanne d'Albret"), his mother, was the Queen of Navarre and niece of King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France"). He was baptized [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), but raised [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"). After his father was killed in 1562, he became [Duke of Vendôme](/wiki/Duke_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Duke of Vendôme") at the age of 10, with Admiral [Gaspard de Coligny](/wiki/Gaspard_II_de_Coligny "Gaspard II de Coligny") (1519–1572) as his regent. Seven years later, the young duke became the nominal leader of the [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") after the death of his uncle the [Prince de Condé](/wiki/Louis_I,_Prince_of_Cond%C3%A9 "Louis I, Prince of Condé") in 1569.

Henry succeeded to Navarre as Henry III when his mother died in 1572. That same year [Catherine de' Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de%27_Medici "Catherine de' Medici"), mother of King [Charles IX of France](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_France "Charles IX of France"), arranged for the marriage of her daughter, [Margaret of Valois](/wiki/Margaret_of_Valois "Margaret of Valois"), to Henry, ostensibly to advance peace between Catholics and Huguenots. Many Huguenots gathered in Paris for the wedding on 24 August, but were ambushed and slaughtered by Catholics in the [St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_Massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre"). Henry saved his own life by converting to Catholicism. He repudiated his conversion in 1576 and resumed his leadership of the Huguenots.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12)[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13)

The period from 1576 to 1584 was relatively calm in France, with the Huguenots consolidating control of much of the south with only occasional interference from the royal government. Extended civil war erupted again in 1584, when [François, Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois,_Duke_of_Anjou "François, Duke of Anjou"), younger brother of King [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"), died, leaving Navarre next in line for the throne. Thus began the [War of the Three Henrys](/wiki/War_of_the_Three_Henrys "War of the Three Henrys"), as Henry of Navarre, Henry III, and the ultra-Catholic leader, [Henry of Guise](/wiki/Henry_I,_Duke_of_Guise "Henry I, Duke of Guise"), fought a confusing three-cornered struggle for dominance. After Henry III was assassinated on 31 July 1589, Navarre claimed the throne as the first Bourbon king of France, Henry IV.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12)[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13)

Much of Catholic France, organized into the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)"), refused to recognize a Protestant monarch and instead recognized Henry IV's uncle, [Charles, Cardinal de Bourbon](/wiki/Charles,_Cardinal_de_Bourbon_(born_1523) "Charles, Cardinal de Bourbon (born 1523)"), as rightful king, and the civil war continued. Henry won a crucial victory at [Ivry](/wiki/Battle_of_Ivry "Battle of Ivry") on 14 March 1590 and, following the death of the Cardinal the same year, the forces of the League lacked an obvious Catholic candidate for the throne and divided into various factions. Nevertheless, as a Protestant, Henry IV was unable to take Paris, a Catholic stronghold, or to decisively defeat his enemies, now supported by the Spanish. He reconverted to Catholicism in 1593[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolt1995147-14) and was crowned king retroactively to 1589 at the [Cathedral of Chartres](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Chartres "Cathedral of Chartres") on 27 February 1594.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnecht2014269-15)

### Early Bourbons in France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early Bourbons in France")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Augustins_-_Henri_IV%2C_roi_de_France_et_de_Navarre_-_Jacques_Boulb%C3%A8ne.jpg/250px-Augustins_-_Henri_IV%2C_roi_de_France_et_de_Navarre_-_Jacques_Boulb%C3%A8ne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Augustins_-_Henri_IV,_roi_de_France_et_de_Navarre_-_Jacques_Boulb%C3%A8ne.jpg)

Henry IV of France, the first Bourbon King of France

Henry granted the [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes") on 13 April 1598, establishing Catholicism as an official state religion but also granting the Huguenots a measure of religious tolerance and political freedom short of full equality with the practice of Catholicism. This compromise ended the religious wars in France. That same year the [Treaty of Vervins](/wiki/Treaty_of_Vervins "Treaty of Vervins") ended the war with Spain, adjusted the Spanish-French border, and resulted in a belated recognition by Spain of Henry as king of France.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Ably assisted by [Maximilien de Béthune, duc de Sully](/wiki/Maximilien_de_B%C3%A9thune,_duc_de_Sully "Maximilien de Béthune, duc de Sully"), Henry reduced the land tax known as the *[taille](/wiki/Taille "Taille")*; promoted agriculture, public works, construction of highways, and the first French canal; started such important industries as the tapestry works of the [Gobelins](/wiki/Gobelins_manufactory "Gobelins manufactory"); and intervened in favor of Protestants in the duchies and earldoms along the German frontier. This last was to be the cause of his assassination.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Henry's marriage to Margaret, which had produced no heir, was annulled in 1599, and he married [Marie de' Medici](/wiki/Marie_de%27_Medici "Marie de' Medici"), niece of the grand duke of Tuscany. A son, Louis, was born to them in 1601. Henry IV was assassinated on 14 May 1610 in Paris. [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII") was only nine years old when he succeeded his father.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) He was to prove a weak ruler; his reign was effectively a series of distinct regimes, depending who held the effective reins of power. At first, Marie de' Medici, his mother, served as regent and advanced a pro-Spanish policy. To deal with the financial troubles of France, Louis summoned the [Estates General](/wiki/Estates_General_(France) "Estates General (France)") in 1614; this would be the last time that body met until the eve of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Marie arranged the 1615 marriage of Louis to [Anne of Austria](/wiki/Anne_of_Austria "Anne of Austria"), the daughter of King [Philip III of Spain](/wiki/Philip_III_of_Spain "Philip III of Spain").

In 1617, Louis conspired with [Charles d'Albert, duc de Luynes](/wiki/Charles_de_Luynes "Charles de Luynes") to dispense with her influence, having her favorite [Concino Concini](/wiki/Concino_Concini "Concino Concini") assassinated on 26 April of that year. After some years of weak government by Louis's favorites, the King made [Armand Jean du Plessis, Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), a former protégé of his mother, the chief minister of France in 1624.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Richelieu advanced an anti-[Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") policy. He arranged for Louis' sister, [Henrietta Maria](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria "Henrietta Maria"), to marry King [Charles I of England](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), on 11 May 1625. Her pro-Catholic propaganda in England was one of the contributing factors to the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). Richelieu, as ambitious for France and the French monarchy as for himself, laid the ground for the [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") that would last in France until the Revolution. He wanted to establish a dominating position for France in Europe, and he wanted to unify France under the monarchy. He established the role of *[intendants](/wiki/Intendant "Intendant")*, non-noble men whose arbitrary powers of administration were granted (and revocable) by the monarch, superseding many of the traditional duties and privileges of the noble governors.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Although it required a succession of internal military campaigns, Richelieu disarmed the fortified Huguenot towns that Henry had allowed. He involved France in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") (1618–1648) against the Habsburgs by [concluding an alliance with Sweden](/wiki/Treaty_of_B%C3%A4rwalde "Treaty of Bärwalde") in 1631 and, actively, in 1635. He died in 1642 before the conclusion of that conflict, having groomed Cardinal [Jules Mazarin](/wiki/Jules_Mazarin "Jules Mazarin") as a successor. Louis XIII outlived him but by one year, dying in 1643 at the age of forty-two. After a childless marriage for twenty-three years his queen, Anne, delivered a son on 5 September 1638, whom he named Louis after himself.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) In the mid-17th century, the Bourbon monarchy had a faulty system for finance and taxation. Their lacking a national bank led to them taking short-term loans, and ordering financial agents to make payments in advance or in excess of tax revenues collected.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Louis XIV and Louis XV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Louis XIV and Louis XV")]

Main articles: [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") and [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Grand_Royal_Coat_of_Arms_of_France_%26_Navarre_%281%29.svg/250px-Grand_Royal_Coat_of_Arms_of_France_%26_Navarre_%281%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Grand_Royal_Coat_of_Arms_of_France_%26_Navarre_(1).svg)

Royal [Coat of arms of the Kingdom of France and Navarre](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_France "Coat of arms of France").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Louis_XIV_of_France_and_his_family_attributed_to_Nicolas_de_Largilli%C3%A8re.jpg/500px-Louis_XIV_of_France_and_his_family_attributed_to_Nicolas_de_Largilli%C3%A8re.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis_XIV_of_France_and_his_family_attributed_to_Nicolas_de_Largilli%C3%A8re.jpg)

Dynastic group portrait of Louis XIV (seated) with his son [Louis the *Grand Dauphin*](/wiki/Louis,_Grand_Dauphin "Louis, Grand Dauphin") (to the left), his grandson [Louis, Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Louis,_Duke_of_Burgundy "Louis, Duke of Burgundy") (to the right), his great-grandson the duc d'Anjou, later Louis XV, and [Madame de Ventadour](/wiki/Madame_de_Ventadour "Madame de Ventadour"), his governess, who commissioned this painting some years later; busts of [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") and [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII") in the background.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Louis14-Family.jpg/500px-Louis14-Family.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louis14-Family.jpg)

A posthumous painting commissioned around 1670 by [Philippe de France](/wiki/Philippe_I,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe I, Duke of Orléans"). It shows the French Bourbon family around that time. It includes: [Henrietta Maria of France](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France") (d. 1669), exiled Queen of England; [Philippe I, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philippe_I,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe I, Duke of Orléans"), founder of the [House of Orléans](/wiki/House_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "House of Orléans"); his first wife [Princess Henriette](/wiki/Henrietta_of_England "Henrietta of England") (d. 1670); the couple's first daughter [Marie Louise d'Orléans](/wiki/Marie_Louise_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans "Marie Louise d'Orléans") (later Queen of Spain); [Anne of Austria](/wiki/Anne_of_Austria "Anne of Austria") (d. 1666); the Orléans daughters of [Gaston, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Gaston,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Gaston, Duke of Orléans"); [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"); the Dauphin of France with his wife [Maria Theresa of Spain](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Spain "Maria Theresa of Spain") with her third daughter [Marie-Thérèse](/wiki/Marie_Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_of_France_(1667%E2%80%931672) "Marie Thérèse of France (1667–1672)"), called [Madame Royale](/wiki/Madame_Royale "Madame Royale") (died 1672) and her second son *Philippe-Charles de France, duc d'Anjou* (d. 1671). The first daughter of Gaston stands on the far right: [Anne Marie Louise d'Orléans](/wiki/Anne_Marie_Louise_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans,_Duchess_of_Montpensier "Anne Marie Louise d'Orléans, Duchess of Montpensier"). The picture frame with the two children are the other two daughters of Louis and Maria Theresa who died in 1662 and 1664.

[Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") succeeded his father at four years of age;[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) he would go on to become the most powerful king in French history. His mother Anne served as his regent with her favorite Jules, Cardinal Mazarin, as chief minister. When Louis was age 7, [Nicolas de Neufville de Villeroy](/wiki/Nicolas_de_Neufville_de_Villeroy "Nicolas de Neufville de Villeroy") became governor of the young king.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevi200437-24) The main childhood places of Louis XIV were the [Palais-Royal](/wiki/Palais-Royal "Palais-Royal") and the nearby [Hôtel de Villeroy](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Villeroy_(Paris,_1st_arrondissement) "Hôtel de Villeroy (Paris, 1st arrondissement)"). Mazarin continued the policies of Richelieu, bringing the Thirty Years' War to a successful conclusion in 1648 and defeating the nobility's challenge to royal absolutism in a series of civil wars known as the [Frondes](/wiki/Fronde "Fronde"). He continued to war with Spain until 1659.

In that year the [Treaty of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees") was signed signifying a major shift in power, France had replaced Spain as the dominant state in Europe. The treaty called for an [arranged marriage](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage") between Louis and his cousin [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Spain "Maria Theresa of Spain"), a daughter of King [Philip IV of Spain](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_Spain "Philip IV of Spain") by his first wife [Elisabeth](/wiki/Elisabeth_of_France_(1602%E2%80%931644) "Elisabeth of France (1602–1644)"), the sister of Louis XIII. They were married in 1660 and had a son, Louis, in 1661.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Mazarin died on 9 March 1661 and it was expected that Louis would appoint another chief minister, as had become the tradition, but instead he shocked the country by announcing he would rule alone.

For six years Louis reformed the finances of his state and built formidable armed forces. France fought a series of wars from 1667 onward and gained some territory on its northern and eastern borders. Maria Theresa died in 1683 and the next year he secretly married the devoutly Catholic [Françoise d'Aubigné, Marquise de Maintenon](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7oise_d%27Aubign%C3%A9,_Marquise_de_Maintenon "Françoise d'Aubigné, Marquise de Maintenon"). Louis XIV began to persecute Protestants, undoing the religious tolerance established by his grandfather Henry IV, culminating in his revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685.

The last war waged by Louis XIV proved to be one of the most important to dynastic Europe. In 1700, King [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain"), a Habsburg, died without a son. Louis's only legitimate son, the *[Grand Dauphin](/wiki/Louis,_Grand_Dauphin "Louis, Grand Dauphin")*, as the late king's nephew, was the closest heir; and Charles willed the kingdom to the Dauphin's second son, the Duke of Anjou. Other powers, particularly the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), who had the next closest claims, objected to such a vast increase in French power.

Initially, most of the other powers were willing to accept Anjou's reign as [Philip V](/wiki/Philip_V_of_Spain "Philip V of Spain"), but Louis's mishandling of their concerns soon drove the [English](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and other powers to join the Austrians in a coalition against France. The [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") began in 1701 and raged for 12 years. In the end Louis's grandson was recognized as king of Spain, but he was obliged to agree to the forfeiture of succession rights in France, the [Spanish Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain")' other European territories were largely ceded to Austria, and France was nearly bankrupted by the cost of the struggle. Louis died on 1 September 1715 ending his seventy-two-year reign, the longest in European history.

The reign of Louis XIV was so long that he outlived both his son and eldest grandson. He was succeeded by his great-grandson [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV").[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) Louis XV was born on 15 February 1710 and was thus aged only five at his ascension, the third Louis in a row to become king of France before the age of thirteen (Louis XIII became king at 9, Louis XIV at almost 5 and himself at 5). Initially, the regency was held by [Philippe, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philippe_II,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe II, Duke of Orléans"), Louis XIV's nephew, as nearest adult male to the throne.[[2]](#cite_note-anselme-3) This *[Régence](/wiki/R%C3%A9gence "Régence")* was seen as a period of greater individual expression, manifested in secular, artistic, literary and colonial activity, in contrast to the austere latter years of Louis XIV's reign.

Following Orléans' death in 1723, the [Duke of Bourbon](/wiki/Louis_Henri,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon"), representative of the Bourbon-Condé cadet line, became prime minister. It was expected that Louis would marry his cousin, the daughter of King Philip V of Spain, but this engagement was broken by the duke in 1725 so that Louis could marry [Marie Leszczyńska](/wiki/Marie_Leszczy%C5%84ska "Marie Leszczyńska"), the daughter of [Stanislas](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Leszczy%C5%84ski "Stanisław Leszczyński"), former king of Poland. Bourbon's motive appears to have been a desire to produce an heir as soon as possible so as to reduce the chances of a succession dispute between Philip V and the Duke of Orléans in the event of the sickly king's death. Maria was already an adult woman at the time of the marriage, while the [infanta](/wiki/Infanta "Infanta") was still a young girl.

Nevertheless, Bourbon's action brought a very negative response from Spain, and for his incompetence Bourbon was soon replaced by Cardinal [André-Hercule de Fleury](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9-Hercule_de_Fleury "André-Hercule de Fleury"), the young king's tutor, in 1726. Fleury was a peace-loving man who intended to keep France out of war, but circumstances presented themselves that made this impossible. The first cause of these wars came in 1733 when [Augustus II](/wiki/Augustus_II_of_Poland "Augustus II of Poland"), the elector of Saxony and king of Poland died. With French support, Stanislas was again elected king. This brought France into conflict with [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") and Austria who supported [Augustus III](/wiki/Augustus_III "Augustus III"), Elector of Saxony and son of Augustus II.

Stanislas lost the Polish crown, but he was given the [Duchy of Lorraine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lorraine "Duchy of Lorraine") as compensation, which would pass to France after his death in 1766. Next came the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") in 1740 in which France supported King [Frederick II of Prussia](/wiki/Frederick_II_of_Prussia "Frederick II of Prussia") against [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa"), [Archduchess of Austria](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Austria "List of rulers of Austria") and [Queen of Hungary](/wiki/List_of_Hungarian_monarchs "List of Hungarian monarchs"). Fleury died in 1743 before the conclusion of the war.

Shortly after Fleury's death in 1745 Louis was influenced by his mistress the [Marquise de Pompadour](/wiki/Marquise_de_Pompadour "Marquise de Pompadour") to reverse the policy of France in 1756 by creating an [alliance](/wiki/Franco-Austrian_Alliance "Franco-Austrian Alliance") with Austria against Prussia in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). The war was a disaster for France, which lost most of her overseas possessions to the British in the [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"). Maria, his wife, died in 1768 and Louis himself died on 10 May 1774.

### French Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: French Revolution")]

Main article: [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution")

[Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") had become the [Dauphin of France](/wiki/Dauphin_of_France "Dauphin of France") upon the death of his father [Louis](/wiki/Louis,_Dauphin_of_France_(1729%E2%80%931765) "Louis, Dauphin of France (1729–1765)"), the son of Louis XV, in 1765. He married [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette") of Austria, a daughter of Holy Roman Empress [Maria Theresa](/wiki/Maria_Theresa "Maria Theresa"), in 1770. Louis intervened in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") against Britain in 1778, but he is most remembered for his role in the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). France was in financial turmoil and Louis was forced to convene the [Estates-General](/wiki/Estates-General_of_1789 "Estates-General of 1789") on 5 May 1789.

They formed the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(French_Revolution) "National Assembly (French Revolution)") and forced Louis to accept a constitution that limited his powers on 14 July 1789. He [tried to flee France](/wiki/Flight_to_Varennes "Flight to Varennes") in June 1791, but was captured. The French monarchy was abolished on 21 September 1792 and a [republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") was proclaimed. The chain of Bourbon monarchs begun in 1589 was broken. Louis XVI was [executed](/wiki/Execution_of_Louis_XVI "Execution of Louis XVI") on 21 January 1793.

Marie Antoinette and her son, Louis, were held as prisoners. Many French royalists proclaimed him [Louis XVII](/wiki/Louis_XVII "Louis XVII"), but he never reigned. She was executed on 16 October 1793. He died of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") on 8 June 1795 at the age of ten while in captivity.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") spread nationalism and anti-absolutism throughout Europe, and the other Bourbon monarchs were threatened. [Ferdinand IV](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Ferdinand I of the Two Sicilies") was forced to flee from Naples in 1806 when [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") deposed him and installed his brother, [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte"), as king. Ferdinand continued to rule from Sicily until 1815.

Napoleon conquered Parma in 1800 and compensated the Bourbon duke with [Etruria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Etruria "Kingdom of Etruria"), a new kingdom he created from the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"). It was short-lived, counting only two monarchs, [Louis](/wiki/Louis_I_of_Etruria "Louis I of Etruria") and [Louis II](/wiki/Charles_II,_Duke_of_Parma "Charles II, Duke of Parma"), as Napoleon annexed Etruria in 1807.

King [Charles IV of Spain](/wiki/Charles_IV_of_Spain "Charles IV of Spain") had been an ally of France. He succeeded his father, [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain"), in 1788. At first he declared war on France on 7 March 1793, but he made peace on 22 June 1795. This peace became an alliance on 19 August 1796. His chief minister, [Manuel de Godoy](/wiki/Manuel_de_Godoy "Manuel de Godoy") convinced Charles that his son, [Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII_of_Spain "Ferdinand VII of Spain"), was plotting to overthrow him. Napoleon exploited the situation and invaded Spain in March 1808. This led to an [uprising](/wiki/Tumult_of_Aranjuez "Tumult of Aranjuez") that forced Charles to abdicate on 19 March 1808 in favor of his son, who became Ferdinand VII. Napoleon forced Ferdinand to return the crown to Charles on 30 April and then convinced Charles to relinquish it to him on 10 May 1808. In turn, he gave it to his brother, Joseph, king of Naples on 6 June 1808. Joseph abandoned Naples to [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat"), the husband of Napoleon's sister. This was very unpopular in Spain and resulted in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"), a struggle that would contribute to the downfall of Napoleon.

### Bourbon Restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Bourbon Restoration")]

Main article: [Bourbon Restoration in France](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Pavillon_royal_de_France.svg/330px-Pavillon_royal_de_France.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pavillon_royal_de_France.svg)

The standard of the French royal family under the *[Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime")* and the [restoration period](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France").

With the abdication of Napoleon on 11 April 1814 the Bourbon dynasty was restored to the Kingdom of France in the person of [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII"), brother of Louis XVI. Napoleon escaped from exile and Louis fled in March 1815. Louis was again restored after the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") on 7 July 1815.

The conservative elements of Europe dominated the post-Napoleonic age, but the values of the French Revolution could not be easily swept aside. Louis granted a [constitution](/wiki/Charter_of_1814 "Charter of 1814") on 14 June 1814 to appease the liberals, but the ultra-royalist party, led by his brother, Charles, continued to influence his reign.[[25]](#cite_note-Durant-26) When he died in 1824 his brother became king as [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France") much to the dismay of French liberals. In a saying ascribed to [Talleyrand](/wiki/Talleyrand "Talleyrand"), "they had learned nothing and forgotten nothing."[[26]](#cite_note-27)

#### Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

Charles passed several laws that appealed to the upper class but angered the middle class. The situation came to a head when he appointed a new minister on 8 August 1829 who did not have the confidence of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(France) "Chamber of Deputies (France)"). The chamber censured the king on 18 March 1830 and in response Charles proclaimed the [July Ordinances](/wiki/July_Ordinances "July Ordinances") on 26 July 1830 intended to silence criticism against him.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This resulted in the [July Revolution](/wiki/July_Revolution "July Revolution").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Coat_of_Arms_of_the_July_Monarchy_%281830-31%29.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_the_July_Monarchy_%281830-31%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_the_July_Monarchy_(1830-31).svg)

Coat of arms of [Louis Philippe](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I") of the [Orléanist](/wiki/Orl%C3%A9anist "Orléanist") cadet branch, French king during the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") 1830–48 (with the revolutionary [Tricolour flag](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France") and the Napoleonic Order of the [Legion of Honour](/wiki/Legion_of_Honour "Legion of Honour"))

As a compromise the crown was offered to [Louis Philippe](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I"), duke of Orléans, a descendant of the [brother of Louis XIV](/wiki/Philippe_I,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe I, Duke of Orléans"), and the head of the Orléanist cadet branch of the Bourbons. Agreeing to reign constitutionally and under the [tricolour](/wiki/Tricolour_(flag) "Tricolour (flag)"), he was proclaimed King of the French on 7 August 1830. The resulting regime, known as the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy"), lasted until the [Revolution of 1848](/wiki/French_Revolution_of_1848 "French Revolution of 1848"). The Bourbon monarchy in France ended on 24 February 1848, when Louis Philippe was forced to abdicate and the short-lived [Second Republic](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic") was established.

Some [Legitimists](/wiki/Legitimists "Legitimists") refused to recognize the Orléanist monarchy. After the death of Charles in 1836 his son was proclaimed [Louis XIX](/wiki/Louis_Antoine,_Duke_of_Angoul%C3%AAme "Louis Antoine, Duke of Angoulême"), though this title was never formally recognized. Charles' grandson [Henri, Count of Chambord](/wiki/Henri,_Count_of_Chambord "Henri, Count of Chambord"), the last Bourbon claimant of the French crown, was proclaimed by some Henry V, but the French monarchy was never restored.

Following the 1870 collapse of the [Second French Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") of Emperor [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III"), Henri was offered a restored throne. However Chambord refused to accept the throne unless France abandoned the revolution-inspired [tricolour](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France") and accepted what he regarded as the true Bourbon flag of France, featuring the [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis"). The tricolour, originally associated with the French Revolution and the [First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), had been used by the July Monarchy, the Second Republic and both Empires; the [French National Assembly](/wiki/French_National_Assembly "French National Assembly") could not possibly agree.

A *temporary* [Third Republic](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") was established, while monarchists waited for the comte de Chambord to die and for the succession to pass to [Prince Philippe, Count of Paris](/wiki/Prince_Philippe,_Count_of_Paris "Prince Philippe, Count of Paris"), who was willing to accept the tricolour. Henri lived until 1883, by which time public opinion had come to accept the republic as the "form of government that divides us least." His death without issue marked the extinction of the main line of the French Bourbons. Thus the head of the House of Bourbon became [Juan, Count of Montizón](/wiki/Juan,_Count_of_Montiz%C3%B3n "Juan, Count of Montizón") of the Spanish line of the house who was also [Carlist](/wiki/Carlist "Carlist") claimant to the throne of Spain, and had become the senior male of the dynasty by primogeniture. His heir as eldest Bourbon and head of the house is today [Louis Alphonse, Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Louis_Alphonse,_Duke_of_Anjou "Louis Alphonse, Duke of Anjou").

However the terms of the Peace of Utrecht forbade the descendants of Philip V of Spain from inheriting the throne of France, therefore many monarchists recognised the Orléans line as heirs to the French throne.

By an ordinance of [Louis Philippe I](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I") of 13 August 1830, it was decided that the king's children (and his sister) would continue to bear the arms of Orléans, that Louis-Philippe's eldest son, as Prince Royal, would bear the title of Duke of Orléans, that the younger sons would continue to have their existing titles, and that the sister and daughters of the king would be styled *Royal Highness* and "d'Orléans," but the Orléans dynasts did not take the name "of France."

## Bourbons of Spain and Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Bourbons of Spain and Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Coat_of_Arms_of_Spanish_Monarch.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Spanish_Monarch.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Spanish_Monarch.svg)

Arms of the current [King of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain") of the House of Bourbon

Main articles: [House of Bourbon-Anjou](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Anjou "House of Bourbon-Anjou") and [Spanish royal family](/wiki/Spanish_royal_family "Spanish royal family")

### Philip V

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Philip V")]

The Spanish branch of the House of Bourbon, also known as the [House of Bourbon-Anjou](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Anjou "House of Bourbon-Anjou"), was founded by [Philip V](/wiki/Philip_V_of_Spain "Philip V of Spain"). He was born in 1683 at Versailles, the second son of the Grand Dauphin, who was eldest son of Louis XIV. He was [Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Duchy_of_Anjou "Duchy of Anjou") and probably never expected to be raised to a rank higher than that. However, King [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain"), dying without issue, willed the throne to his grand-nephew the Duke of Anjou, who was the younger grandson of his eldest sister [Marie-Thérèse](/wiki/Maria_Theresa_of_Spain "Maria Theresa of Spain"), who had married Louis XIV.

The prospect of Bourbons on both the French and Spanish thrones was resisted as creating an [imbalance of power](/wiki/European_balance_of_power "European balance of power") in Europe by its powers and, upon Charles II's death on 1 November 1700, a [Grand Alliance](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_(League_of_Augsburg) "Grand Alliance (League of Augsburg)") of European nations united against Philip. This was known as the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"). In the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht"), signed on 11 April 1713, Philip was recognized as [king of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain") but his renunciation of succession rights to France was affirmed and, of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire")'s other European territories, Sicily was [ceded to Savoy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily_under_Savoy "Kingdom of Sicily under Savoy"), and the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), [Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan"), and Naples were allotted to the [Austrian Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy").

Philip had two sons by his first wife. After her death, he married [Elisabeth Farnese](/wiki/Elisabeth_Farnese "Elisabeth Farnese"), niece of [Francesco Farnese, Duke of Parma](/wiki/Francesco_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Francesco Farnese, Duke of Parma"), in 1714. She presented Philip with three sons, for whom she had ambitions of securing Italian crowns. She therefore induced Philip to [occupy Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1700%E2%80%931720) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1700–1720)") and Sicily in 1717.

A [Quadruple Alliance](/wiki/Quadruple_Alliance_(1718) "Quadruple Alliance (1718)") of Britain, France, Austria and the Netherlands was organized on 2 August 1718 to stop him. In the [Treaty of The Hague](/wiki/Treaty_of_The_Hague_(1720) "Treaty of The Hague (1720)"), signed on 17 February 1720, Philip renounced his conquests of Sardinia and Sicily, but he assured the ascension of his eldest son by Elisabeth to the Duchy of Parma upon the reigning duke's death. Philip abdicated in January 1724 in favor of [Louis I](/wiki/Louis_of_Spain "Louis of Spain"), his eldest son with his first wife, but Louis died in August and Philip resumed the crown.

When the [War of the Polish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Polish_Succession "War of the Polish Succession") began in 1733, Philip and Elisabeth saw another opportunity to advance the claims of their sons and recover at least part of the former possessions of the Spanish crown on the Italian peninsula. Philip signed the [Family Compact](/wiki/Pacte_de_Famille "Pacte de Famille") with Louis XV, his nephew and king of France. [Charles](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain"), Duke of Parma since 1731, invaded Naples. At the conclusion of peace on 13 November 1738, control of Parma and Piacenza was ceded to Austria, which had occupied the duchies but was now forced to recognise Charles as king of Naples and Sicily. Philip also used the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") to win back control of Parma. He did not live to see it to its conclusion, however, dying in 1746.

### Ferdinand VI and Charles III

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ferdinand VI and Charles III")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Ferdinand_VI_of_Bourbon_coat_of_arms.jpg/250px-Ferdinand_VI_of_Bourbon_coat_of_arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ferdinand_VI_of_Bourbon_coat_of_arms.jpg)

Arms of [Ferdinand VI](/wiki/Ferdinand_VI "Ferdinand VI") at the [Omoa Fortress](/wiki/Fortaleza_de_San_Fernando "Fortaleza de San Fernando") in Honduras

[Ferdinand VI](/wiki/Ferdinand_VI "Ferdinand VI"), second son of Philip V and his first wife, succeeded his father. He was a peace-loving monarch who kept Spain out of the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). He died in 1759 in the midst of that conflict and was succeeded by his half-brother [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain"), already reigning as king in Naples and Sicily.

Following Spain's victory over the Austrians at the [Battle of Bitonto](/wiki/Battle_of_Bitonto "Battle of Bitonto"), it proved inexpedient to reunite Naples and Sicily to Spain, so as a compromise Charles became King of Naples, as Charles IV and VII of Sicily. Following Charles' accession to the Spanish throne in 1759, he was required, by the Treaty of Naples of 3 October 1759, to abdicate Naples and Sicily to his third son, [Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Ferdinand I of the Two Sicilies"), thus initiating the branch known as the [Neapolitan Bourbons](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies").

Charles revived the Family Compact with France on 15 August 1761 and joined in the Seven Years' War against Britain in 1762; the reformist policies he had espoused in Naples were pursued with similar energy in Spain, where he completely overhauled the cumbersome bureaucracy of the state. As a French ally, he opposed Britain during the American Revolution in June 1779, supplying large quantities of weapons and munitions to the rebels and keeping one third of all the British forces in the Americas occupied defending Florida and what is now [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama"), which were ultimately recaptured by Spain. Charles died in 1788.

### Bourbons of Parma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Bourbons of Parma")]

Elisabeth Farnese's ambitions were realized at the conclusion of the War of the Austrian Succession in 1748 when the [Duchy of Parma and Piacenza](/wiki/Duchy_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duchy of Parma and Piacenza"), already occupied by Spanish troops, were ceded by Austria to her second son, [Philip](/wiki/Philip_of_Parma "Philip of Parma"), and combined with the former [Duchy of Guastalla](/wiki/Duchy_of_Guastalla "Duchy of Guastalla") of the [Gonzagas](/wiki/House_of_Gonzaga "House of Gonzaga"). Elisabeth died in 1766.

Arms of the [House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Coat_of_arms_of_the_House_of_Bourbon-Parma.svg/250px-Coat_of_arms_of_the_House_of_Bourbon-Parma.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_the_House_of_Bourbon-Parma.svg)

Arms of the [House of Bourbon-Parma](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Parma "House of Bourbon-Parma")

Upon the fall of the French Empire, Ferdinand I was restored to his throne in Naples, forming the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies in 1816 and founding the [House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies"). His subjects revolted in 1820 and he was forced to grant a constitution; [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") invaded in March 1821 at his request and revoked the constitution. He was succeeded by his son, [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Francis I of the Two Sicilies"), in 1825 and by his grandson, [Ferdinand II](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Ferdinand II of the Two Sicilies"), in 1830. A [revolution in Sicily](/wiki/Sicilian_revolution_of_1848 "Sicilian revolution of 1848") erupted in January 1848 and Ferdinand was also forced to grant a constitution. This constitution was revoked in 1849. Ferdinand was succeeded by his son, [Francis II](/wiki/Francis_II_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Francis II of the Two Sicilies"), in May 1859.

When [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") captured Naples in 1860, Francis restored the constitution in an attempt to save his sovereignty. He fled to the fortress of [Gaeta](/wiki/Gaeta "Gaeta"), which was besieged and [captured by the Piedmontese troops](/wiki/Siege_of_Gaeta_(1861) "Siege of Gaeta (1861)") in February 1861. His kingdom was incorporated into the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") on 17 March 1861, after the fall the fortress of [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina") (surrendered on 12 March), although the Neapolitan troops in [Civitella del Tronto](/wiki/Civitella_del_Tronto "Civitella del Tronto") resisted three days longer.

After the fall of Napoleon, Napoleon's wife, [Maria Louisa](/wiki/Marie_Louise,_Duchess_of_Parma "Marie Louise, Duchess of Parma"), was made Duchess of Parma. As compensation, Charles Louis, the former king of Etruria, was made the [Duke of Lucca](/wiki/Duchy_of_Lucca "Duchy of Lucca"). When Maria Louisa died in 1847 he was restored to Parma as [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Parma "Charles II of Parma"). Lucca was incorporated into Tuscany. He was succeeded by his son, [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Parma "Charles III of Parma"), and grandson, [Robert I](/wiki/Robert_I_of_Parma "Robert I of Parma"), in 1854. The people of Parma voted for a union with the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") in 1860. After Italian unification the next year, the Bourbon dynasty in Italy was no more.

Ferdinand VII was restored to the throne of Spain in March 1814. Like his Italian Bourbon counterpart, his subjects [revolted](/wiki/Trienio_Liberal "Trienio Liberal") against him in January 1820 and he was forced to grant a constitution. A [French army](/wiki/Hundred_Thousand_Sons_of_Saint_Louis "Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis") invaded in 1823 and the constitution was revoked. Ferdinand married his fourth wife, [Maria Christina](/wiki/Maria_Christina_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "Maria Christina of Bourbon-Two Sicilies"), the daughter of Francis I, the Bourbon king of the Two Sicilies, in 1829. Despite his many marriages he did not have a son, so in 1833 he was influenced by his wife to abolish the [Salic Law](/wiki/Salic_Law "Salic Law") so that their daughter, Isabella, could become queen depriving his brother, [Don Carlos](/wiki/Infante_Carlos,_Count_of_Molina "Infante Carlos, Count of Molina"), of the throne.

[Isabella II](/wiki/Isabella_II_of_Spain "Isabella II of Spain") succeeded her father when he died in 1833. She was only three years old and Maria Cristina, her mother, served as regent. Maria knew that she needed the support of the liberals to oppose Don Carlos so she granted a constitution in 1834. Don Carlos found his [greatest support](/wiki/Carlism "Carlism") in [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") and the [Basque country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(greater_region) "Basque Country (greater region)") because the constitution centralized the provinces thus denying them the autonomy they sought. He was defeated and fled the country in 1839. Isabella was declared of age in 1843 and she married her cousin [Francisco de Asís](/wiki/Francisco_de_As%C3%ADs,_Duke_of_C%C3%A1diz "Francisco de Asís, Duke of Cádiz"), the son of her father's brother, on 10 October 1846. A military revolution broke out against Isabella in 1868 and she was deposed on 29 September 1868. She abdicated in favor of her son, Alfonso, in 1870, but Spain was proclaimed a republic for a brief time.

When the [First Spanish Republic](/wiki/First_Spanish_Republic "First Spanish Republic") failed, the crown was offered to Isabella's son, who accepted on 1 January 1875 as [Alfonso XII](/wiki/Alfonso_XII "Alfonso XII"). The Carlist pretender, [Don Carlos](/wiki/Carlos,_Duke_of_Madrid "Carlos, Duke of Madrid"), who returned to Spain, was defeated and resumed his exile in February 1876. Alfonso granted a new [constitution](/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1876 "Spanish Constitution of 1876") in July 1876 that was more liberal than the one granted by his grandmother. His reign was cut short when he died in 1885 at the age of twenty-eight.

[Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII") was born on 17 May 1886, after the death of his father. His mother, [Maria Christina](/wiki/Maria_Christina_of_Austria "Maria Christina of Austria"), the second wife of Alfonso XII, served as regent. Alfonso XIII was declared of age in 1902, and he married [Victoria Eugenie of Battenberg](/wiki/Victoria_Eugenie_of_Battenberg "Victoria Eugenie of Battenberg"), the granddaughter of the British [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), on 31 May 1906. He remained neutral during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), but he supported the military coup of [Miguel Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Miguel_Primo_de_Rivera "Miguel Primo de Rivera") on 13 September 1923. A movement towards the establishment of a republic began in 1930, and Alfonso fled the country on 14 April 1931. He never formally abdicated, but he lived the rest of his life in exile and died in 1941.

The Bourbon dynasty seemed finished in Spain as in the rest of the world, but it would be resurrected. The [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") was overthrown in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), leading to the dictatorship of [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"). He named [Juan Carlos de Borbón](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I_of_Spain "Juan Carlos I of Spain"), a grandson of Alfonso XIII, his successor in 1969. When Franco died six years later, Juan Carlos I took the throne to restore the Bourbon dynasty. The new king oversaw the [Spanish transition to democracy](/wiki/Spanish_transition_to_democracy "Spanish transition to democracy"), and the [Spanish Constitution of 1978](/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1978 "Spanish Constitution of 1978") recognized the monarchy.

Since 1964, the Bourbon-Parma line has reigned agnatically (although not officially) in Luxembourg through Grand Dukes [Jean](/wiki/Jean_of_Luxembourg "Jean of Luxembourg") and his son [Henri](/wiki/Henri_of_Luxembourg "Henri of Luxembourg"). In June 2011, Luxembourg adopted absolute primogeniture, replacing the old Semi-Salic law that might have guaranteed the survival of Bourbon rule for generations. Although it is not as powerful as it once was and no longer reigns in its native country of France, the House of Bourbon is by no means extinct and has survived to the present-day world, predominantly composed of republics.

The House of Bourbon, in its surviving branches, is believed to be the oldest royal dynasty of Europe (and the oldest documented European family altogether) that is still existing in the direct male line today. The [House of Capet](/wiki/House_of_Capet "House of Capet")'s male ancestors, the [Robertians](/wiki/Robertians "Robertians"), go back to [Robert of Hesbaye](/wiki/Robert_of_Hesbaye "Robert of Hesbaye") (d. 807) as their first secured ancestor, and he is believed to be a direct male descendant of Charibert de Haspengau (c. 555–636). Should this be true, only the [Imperial House of Japan](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") would outmatch the Bourbon's age, being reliably documented – as a ruling house already – from about 540. The [House of Hesse](/wiki/House_of_Hesse "House of Hesse") traces its line back to 841, the [House of Welf-Este](/wiki/House_of_Welf "House of Welf") and the [House of Wettin](/wiki/House_of_Wettin "House of Wettin") were both emerging in the 10th century, and so were some Italian non-ruling houses like the [Caetani](/wiki/Caetani "Caetani") or the [Massimo family](/wiki/Massimo_family "Massimo family"), whereas most of the other ruling families of Europe only turned up to the light of history after the year 1000.

## Surnames used

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Surnames used")]

Officially, the [King of France](/wiki/King_of_France "King of France") had no family name. A prince with the rank of *[fils de France](/wiki/Fils_de_France "Fils de France")* (Son of France) is surnamed "*de France*"; all the male-line descendants of each *fils de France*, however, took his main title (whether an [appanage](/wiki/Appanage "Appanage") or a [courtesy title](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title")) as their family or last name. However, when [Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") was put on trial and later "[guillotined](/wiki/Guillotined "Guillotined")" (executed) by the revolutionaries [National Convention](/wiki/National_Convention "National Convention") in France in 1793, they somewhat contemptuously referred to him in written documents and spoken address as "Citizen Louis Capet" as if a "commoner" (referring back to the [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") origins of the Bourbon Dynasty's name and referring to [Hugh Capet](/wiki/Hugh_Capet "Hugh Capet"), founder of the [Capetian dynasty](/wiki/Capetian_dynasty "Capetian dynasty")).

Members of the House of Bourbon-Condé and its cadet branches, which never ascended to the throne, used the surname "de Bourbon" until their extinction in 1830.

The daughters of [Gaston, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Gaston,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Gaston, Duke of Orléans"), were the first members of the House of Bourbon since the accession of Henry IV to take their surname from the appanage of their father (d'Orléans). Gaston died without a male heir; his titles reverted to the crown. It was given to his nephew, [Philippe I, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philippe_I,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philippe I, Duke of Orléans"), brother of Louis XIV, whose descendants still bear the surname.

When Philippe, grandson of Louis XIV, became King of Spain as [Philip V](/wiki/Philip_V_of_Spain "Philip V of Spain"), he gave up his French titles. As a Son of France, his actual surname was "de France". However, since that surname was not heritable for descendants of rank lower than Son of France, and since Philippe had already given up his French titles, his descendants simply took the name of their royal house as their surname ("de Bourbon", rendered in Spanish as "*de Borbón*").

The children of Philippe's brother, [Charles, Duke of Berry](/wiki/Charles,_Duke_of_Berry_(1686%E2%80%931714) "Charles, Duke of Berry (1686–1714)") (all of whom died in infancy), were given the surname "d'Alençon". He was Duke of Berry only [in name](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title"), so the surname of his children was taken from his first substantial duchy.

The children of Charles Philippe, Count of Artois, brother of Louis XVI, were surnamed "d'Artois". When Charles succeeded to the throne as [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_of_France "Charles X of France"), his son [Louis Antoine](/wiki/Louis_Antoine,_Duke_of_Angoul%C3%AAme "Louis Antoine, Duke of Angoulême") became a Son of France, with the corresponding change in surname. His grandson, [Henri d'Artois](/wiki/Henry,_Count_of_Chambord "Henry, Count of Chambord"), being merely a Grandson of France, would use the surname until his death.

## The three dynasties of Bourbon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: The three dynasties of Bourbon")]

The first were the lords of Bourbon, who died out by the males in 1171, then by the women in 1216. Their coat of arms are: D'or au lion de gueules, et à l'orle de huit coquilles d'azur Nicolas Louis Achaintre, *Genealogical and chronological history of the royal house of Bourbon*, vol. 1, [éd. Didot](/wiki/Didot_family "Didot family"), 1825, p. 45.

The second family formed by the marriage of the last descendant of the first family, [Mathilde of Bourbon](/wiki/Mathilde_of_Bourbon "Mathilde of Bourbon") with [Guy II of Dampierre](/wiki/Guy_II_of_Dampierre "Guy II of Dampierre"), this land passed to the [house of Dampierre](/wiki/House_of_Dampierre "House of Dampierre") in 1196. The coat of arms of this family is: "De gueules à deux léopards d'or, avec couronne de baron",[[27]](#cite_note-28) but they took the coat of arms of the previous ones. The son of Guy de Dampierre and Mahaut de Bourbon, Archambaud VIII, took the name and arms of his mother, "de Bourbon", the [House of Bourbon-Dampierre](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Dampierre "House of Bourbon-Dampierre"). By the marriage of, [Agnes of Dampierre](/wiki/Agnes_of_Dampierre "Agnes of Dampierre") (died around 1287), with [John of Burgundy](/wiki/John_of_Burgundy_(1231%E2%80%931268) "John of Burgundy (1231–1268)"), this important lordship passed to their daughter [Béatrice de Bourgogne](/wiki/Beatrice_of_Burgundy,_Lady_of_Bourbon "Beatrice of Burgundy, Lady of Bourbon") (1257–1310), lady of Bourbon, then to her husband [Robert, Count of Clermont](/wiki/Robert,_Count_of_Clermont "Robert, Count of Clermont") (1256–1317), and penultimate child of [Saint Louis](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France"), thus possessing the land of Bourbon by "the right of the woman (*[de iure uxoris](/wiki/De_iure_uxoris "De iure uxoris")*).

The third house of Bourbon acceded to the throne of [Navarre](/wiki/Lower_Navarre "Lower Navarre") in 1555, then to the throne of France in 1589 by [Henri IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France"). His coat of arms are: "D'azur, fleurs-de-lys d'or sans nombre, l'écu brisé d'un bâton ou cotice de gueules, brochant sur le tout, avec couronne de fils de France. The name *House of Bourbon* was then used to describe the entire [House of France](/wiki/House_of_France "House of France"), officially since 29 June 1768, date of death of [Hélène de Courtenay](/w/index.php?title=H%C3%A9l%C3%A8ne_de_Courtenay&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hélène de Courtenay (page does not exist)") (1689–1768), with which was extinguished the [Capetian House of Courtenay](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Courtenay "Capetian House of Courtenay"), extinction which made the House of France the only branch dynasty resulting from the dukes of Bourbon.

## Second House of Bourbon (Bourbon-Dampierre)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=House_of_Bourbon&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Second House of Bourbon (Bourbon-Dampierre)")]

Main article: [House of Bourbon-Dampierre](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Dampierre "House of Bourbon-Dampierre")

Prince of Bourbon since 1196:

* [Mathilde](/wiki/Mathilde_of_Bourbon "Mathilde of Bourbon") (Mahaut), Lady of Bourbon († 1218), granddaughter of Archambault VII, Lord of Bourbon, married [Guy II of Dampierre, Marshall of Champagne](/wiki/Guy_II_of_Dampierre "Guy II of Dampierre") († 1216)
  + [Archambaud VIII the Great, Lord of Bourbon](/wiki/Archambaud_VIII_of_Bourbon "Archambaud VIII of Bourbon") († 1242)
    - [Archambaud IX the Young, Lord of Bourbon](/wiki/Archambaud_IX_of_Bourbon "Archambaud IX of Bourbon") († 1249), married Yolande of Châtillon, Countess of Nevers, Auxerre and Tonnerre
      * Mahaut II, Lady of Bourbon († 1262) married [Eudes of Burgundy](/wiki/Odo,_Count_of_Nevers "Odo, Count of Nevers") († 1266)
        + [Capetian House of Burgundy](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Burgundy "Capetian House of Burgundy")
      * Agnès, Countess of Nevers, Auxerre and Tonnerre († 1288) married [John of Burgundy](/wiki/John_of_Burgundy_(1231%E2%80%931268) "John of Burgundy (1231–1268)"), Lord of Charolais († 1268)
        + [Beatrix of Burgundy](/wiki/Beatrice_of_Burgundy,_Lady_of_Bourbon "Beatrice of Burgundy, Lady of Bourbon") married [Robert, Count of Clermont](/wiki/Robert,_Count_of_Clermont "Robert, Count of Clermont")
